summary: High‑energy second set with scorching Women>Eyes and monster Other One
blurb: The first set rolls steadily before the second set erupts with a blazing Women → Eyes transition, a massive The Other One jam, and crisp renditions of Box of Rain and Althea. Brent’s keyboards soar, Jerry’s tone is razor‑sharp, and the matrix mix captures every nuance.
review:
On September 12, 1988 the Dead took the Spectrum in Philadelphia for a night that started solidly and finished with fireworks. The opening set featured reliable work from the core catalog – Jack Straw, Althea, Never Trust A Woman and Dire Wolf – but it was the second set that truly ignited the crowd. A razor‑sharp segue from Man Smart, Woman Smarter into Eyes Of The World launched a high‑octane Women → Eyes sequence, followed by an expansive post‑Space jam that birthed one of the night’s monsters: The Other One. Brent Mydland’s keyboards were “awesome,” weaving soaring leads through the maelstrom while Phil Lesh laid down thunderous bass chops on Eyes and The Other One. Jerry Garcia’s guitar tone was described as crisp and powerful, his solo on The Other One a highlight, and his vocals noticeably tighter than earlier in the show. Good Lovin’ closed the set with a playful vocal rap that left the audience grinning.

Among the recordings available, the Nawrocki matrix (gd88-09-12.matrix.nawrocki.11265.sbeok.shnf) stands out as the definitive source: an excellent, sharp mix that balances the band’s dynamics and preserves the energy of those key moments without distracting bleed. Listeners seeking the clearest window into this high‑energy Philadelphia show should start there.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are clearly positive, praising Women>Eyes, The Other One, Box of Rain, Althea and Brent's keyboards; one recording is mixed but still notes strong moments. Consistent high praise across multiple sources warrants a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Sharp, well‑balanced matrix mix with clear instrument separation; captures the iconic Women → Eyes transition and monster Other One without distracting bleed.

key_highlights: Women → Eyes segue ignites the second set and sustains high energy.
A monster The Other One jam showcases Brent’s soaring keyboards and Phil’s bass chops.
Box of Rain and Althea deliver crisp, powerful performances; Good Lovin’ adds playful vocal rap.
Sharp matrix recording provides excellent clarity and balance for the entire show.

band_performance.Jerry: Tone crisp and powerful; standout solo on The Other One; vocals tighter than earlier in the set though some early moments sounded sluggish.
band_performance.Phil: Strong bass chops, especially on Eyes and The Other One; visible pre‑set smoking adds to the vibe; drives post‑Space jam with authority.
band_performance.Bob: Distorted vocals add character on Women → Eyes; playful rap in Good Lovin’; solid rhythm work despite a minor critique of Jack Straw timing.
band_performance.Brent: Awesome keyboard solos throughout, especially during The Other One; solid support and vocal contributions on Women → Eyes.
band_performance.Mickey: Generally mild‑mannered drumming; provides steady pulse without standout issues.
band_performance.Billy: Steady, supportive drumming complementing Mickey; no major criticisms noted.
